english: pf. bral (bral), imp. brol (brol), intrs. of o_p'rál-ba ('phral ba), to be separated, parted from, deprived of, c. daṅ (dang), e.g. from one's retinue, of the light of doctrine Dzl.; o_brál-bar mi p'od bú-mo k'yod ('bral bar mi phod bu mo khyod) thou, my daughter, from whom I am not able to part Glr.; ćuṅ-ṅu-nas p'a-má gnyis daṅ brál-te (chung ngu nas pha ma gnyis dang bral te) from a child bereft of parents, an orphan from infancy Pth.; to lose, to be bereft, frq. used in reference to the death of near relations; mdo-sdé daṅ lág-pa mi brál-żiṅ (mdo sde dang lag pa mi bral zhing) as the sacred writings never came out of his hands; skóm-pa daṅ brál-bar gyúr-to (skom pa dang bral bar gyur to) he got rid of his thirst; nad daṅ brál-bar gyúr-to (nad dang bral bar gyur to) he recovered from his illness, frq. (in such cases often confounded by the illiterate with nád-las bsgral (nad las bsgral) etc.); more particularly: srog daṅ (srog dang) etc. to die, perish, frq.; o_jíg-ciṅ o_brál-bar gyúr-ba ('jig cing 'bral bar gyur ba) to be dissolved, of the human body Dzl.; o_dú-ba yód-na o_bral-bar oṅ ('du ba yod na 'bral bar 'ong) what was solid, is dissolved in dust Dzl.; o_bral(-bar) med(-pa) ('bral (-bar) med (-pa)) inseparable, indissoluble, frq.

C. also o_brás-mo ('bras mo), resp. bzaṅ-o_brás (bzang 'bras) (Pur. *bras*, Ld. *das*, Lh. *dai*, C. *de*) 1. rice; o_bras-dkár(-mo) ('bras dkar (-mo)) white rice, o_bras-dmár ('bras dmar) red rice (the inferior and cheaper sort); of the former there seem to be distinguished: o_bo-tsa-li ('bo tsa li) (Hd. *basmati*), rgyal-mo-ysáṅ (rgyal mo ysang), ham-dzém (ham dzem), o_dzin-o_dzin ('dzin 'dzin) the second sort, acc. to Cs.; o_brás-kyi srus ('bras kyi srus) peeled rice Sch.; o_bras-sá-lu ('bras sa lu) 'wild rice' Sch.; o_bras-so-ba ('bras so ba) Sch. and Schr., rice not husked o_brás-mo spos-śel ('bras mo spos shel) or dkar-o_dzóm (dkar 'dzom) Ts. maize. Comp. o_bras-ćáṅ ('bras chang) rice-wine, rice-beer. -- o_bras-ćán ('bras chan) boiled rice. -- śa-o_brás (sha 'bras) rice mixed with small pieces of meat. -- o_bras-túg ('bras tug) rice-soup. -- o_bras-żiṅ ('bras zhing) rice-field. -- o_bras-zán ('bras zan) dish of rice. -- o_bras-yós ('bras yos) parched rice Med. -- o_bras-sil ('bras sil) C. boiled rice, got up with butter, sugar, apricots etc., W. *p'u-lá, p'o-ló*, پُلاو . -- 2. tumour, esp. larger swellings in the groin etc.

1. fruit, e.g. śiṅ-gi (shing gi) Mil.; o_brás-bu ye-méd-kyi sa ('bras bu ye med kyi sa) a country producing no fruit Thgy.; corn, grain, o_brás-bu zór-bas brṅá-ba ('bras bu zor bas brnga ba) Mil.; o_bras-ṅan ('bras ngan) a failure of fruit. -- 2. testicle Wdn. cf. rlíg-pa (rlig pa); míg-o_brás (mig 'bras) apple of the eye. -- 3. fig. effect, consequence, esp. as opp. to rgyu (rgyu), hence rgyu-o_brás (rgyu 'bras) cause and effect, more esp. in moral philosophy = retribution, requital, recompense, reward, three grades being distinguished: 1. rnám-par smín-pai o_brás-bu (rnam par smin pai 'bras bu) full recompense, in the worst case by the punishments of hell; 2. rgyu mt'ún-pai o_brás-bu (rgyu mthun pai 'bras bu) by adversity during life; 3. dbáṅ-gi o_brás-bu (dbang gi 'bras bu) by unpleasant local circumstances, -- so Thgy.; rgyu-o_brás (rgyu 'bras) and o_brás-bu ('bras bu) also directly denote the doctrine of final retribution, o_brás-bu mi bden ('bras bu mi bden) the doctrine of requital is not true Thgy.; further: o_brás-bu ('bras bu) reward of ascetic exercises, the various grades of perfection, of which four are distinguished: a. rgyún-du-żugs-pa (rgyun du zhugs pa) श्रोतापत्ति or as partic. °पन्न, he who enters the stream (that takes from the external world to Nirwana); b. lan-gcíg-p'yír-oṅ-ba (lan gcig phyir 'ong ba) सकृदागामिन्, he who returns once more (for the period of a human birth); c. p'yír-mi-oṅ-ba (phyir mi 'ong ba) अनागामिन् he who returns no more, being a candidate of Nirwana; d. dgra-bcóm-pa (dgra bcom pa) अर्हत्, the Arhat, the finished saint; v. Köpp. I, 398.
or གུང་ o_bri-k'uṅ ('bri khung) or -guṅ (-gung) sect of Lamas and monastery in Tibet, o_bri-k'uṅ-pa ('bri khung pa) member of that sect.
a form of medicine, prob. a kind of extract Med.; o_bri-ta-sa-o_dzin ('bri ta sa 'dzin) medicinal herb, an emetic, Med.; in Lh. Cuscuta, which however does not agree with the descriptions.

I. pf. and imp. bri (bri), intrs. of o_p'rí-ba ('phri ba) to lessen, decrease, diminish, of water, frq. in conjunction with k'a (kha), at the surface, used with regard to size, number and intensity (synon. o_grib-pa ('grib pa)) -- II. pf. and imp. bris (bris) (Glr. also bri (bri)) 1. to draw, design, describe, dkyil-o_k'ór żig (dkyil 'khor zhig) to describe a circle or other figure; also to paint Glr. 2. to write, yi-ge (yi ge) letters, a letter (epistle); yi-ger (yi ger) 'literis mandare', to record, to write down, something from hearing Dzl.: o_bri-smyúg ('bri smyug) writing-reed, pen, pencil etc.
